metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete verification step for AC1 to the Verification section, e.g. a CI environment variable check, a required ticket label, or a guard assertion in compact.py that reads the attestation flag, so an executor can mechanically confirm the gate before merging step 3.
findings.finding: AC1 (operator-attested readiness gate) is not covered by any proving command in the Verification section. The plan records it as a 'ticket attestation,' but an executing agent has no deterministic step to verify the gate is satisfied before proceeding to step 3 (the write-side drop).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add per-call-site coverage to AC3 (or a new AC) requiring that each of the three migrated files (`signing._record_for_kind`, `validate.py:signature_findings`, `txn.py:_signature_gate`) is tested against a mirror-less snapshot to confirm the wiring, not just that the helper itself resolves correctly.
findings.finding: The three reader-migration deliverables — `signing._record_for_kind`, `validate.py:signature_findings`, and `txn.py:_signature_gate` — are described as independent wiring changes (What, step 2), but no acceptance criterion verifies that each call site was actually updated. AC3(b) tests the `most_recent_attestation` helper in isolation; the plan explicitly states 'one accessor test covers all three call sites,' meaning a failure to migrate any of the three files would not be caught by the specified tests.
findings.location: AC section, AC3(b); What section step 2
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3(c) or add AC3(e): 'a mirror-less snapshot passed to each of the three migrated call sites (`_record_for_kind` kind=None, `signature_findings`, `_signature_gate`) resolves without KeyError, verifying the wiring — not just the helper.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_attestations_integration.py` to the scope/edit list and update line 97's assertion to reflect that a default-compacted SNAPSHOT omits `signature` (or parameterize it with the rollback flag).
findings.finding: tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_attestations_integration.py line 97 asserts `assert 'attestations' in snap and 'signature' in snap` — it directly pins that a new SNAPSHOT compiled_state contains the legacy `signature` mirror. After step 3 (compact.py drops `signature` by default), this assertion will break. The plan does not enumerate this test as a consumer requiring update.
findings.suggested_fix: In test_attestations_integration.py, change the assertion at line 97 from `assert 'attestations' in snap and 'signature' in snap` to `assert 'attestations' in snap` (and add a separate parameterized test that enables the rollback flag to verify the mirror is re-emitted when `emit_legacy_signature_mirror=true`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fully specify the `signing.most_recent_attestation(state)` contract in the plan: return type (`dict | None`), guard against non-dict `state['attestations']`, guard against `None`/non-numeric `signed_at` (treat as 0 or skip?), and clarify the tie-break definition ('last processed' = last in what order — dict insertion order, or event-file HLC sort order — and confirm it is byte-equivalent to the legacy mirror's file-sort LWW).
findings.finding: `signing.most_recent_attestation(state)` is named as the central new accessor that all three migrated readers call, and AC3b asserts that one test of this function covers all three call sites — but the function does not exist in `signing.py` and its full contract is only partially described in prose. Missing: return type, handling of a non-dict `state['attestations']`, handling of records with `None`/non-numeric `signed_at`, and the exact tie-breaking semantics (the legacy mirror uses file-sort order; `most_recent_attestation` is specified to use `signed_at` — these are NOT equivalent when two records share the same `signed_at` but have different manifest kinds).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the post-rollback schema state for already-stripped SNAPSHOTs: document whether a `rebar compact-all` (or `compact-all --force`) must be run after toggling `emit_legacy_signature_mirror = true` to backfill `signature` on already-stripped SNAPSHOTs, and add this step to the rollback procedure in `docs/migrations.md`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts rollback is 'reversible with NO code downgrade' by toggling `emit_legacy_signature_mirror = true`, which re-emits the mirror on future compactions — but SNAPSHOTs already written WITHOUT `signature` remain stripped in persisted storage until each ticket is recompacted. `process_snapshot` restores `compiled_state` verbatim, so a rolled-back environment still serves `state['signature'] = None` for all tickets whose last SNAPSHOT was written during the drop window. The plan never asserts the schema state of already-persisted SNAPSHOTs after rollback, nor whether a follow-up `compact-all --force` or equivalent is required to backfill the mirror on those tickets.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Rollback + named target version AC; 'What' step 3
findings.suggested_fix: In the rollback AC and `docs/migrations.md`, add an explicit post-rollback remediation step: after setting `emit_legacy_signature_mirror = true`, run `rebar compact-all` to recompact all tickets and re-emit `signature` in their SNAPSHOTs. Alternatively, note that `_processors.py`'s fold-in already backfills `attestations` from a legacy `signature` on read — document that the analogous forward direction (re-emitting `signature` from `attestations` on read) is NOT performed and therefore a forced recompaction is the only recovery path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the tie-breaking rule for equal `signed_at` values in `most_recent_attestation`: e.g. 'use dict insertion order (Python 3.7+), meaning the kind written last to the attestations map wins' — matching exactly how the legacy mirror was last-written.
findings.finding: The tie-breaking rule for `most_recent_attestation` when two records share the same `signed_at` timestamp is described as 'ties → last processed, matching the legacy mirror's last-writer-wins,' but 'last processed' is undefined in the context of a kind-keyed dict. An executor cannot know whether this means dict insertion order, iteration order of the attestations map at write time, or some other ordering, without investigating the existing mirror logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the step 1 description: 'ties broken by dict insertion order of the `attestations` map (Python 3.7+ guarantee), i.e. the kind whose entry was inserted last wins — replicating the last-writer-wins behavior of the legacy reducer.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the tie-break rule for `most_recent_attestation` precisely: when two records share equal `signed_at`, use the record whose event sorts last by HLC filename order (matching the legacy mirror's replay-order last-writer-wins), or document explicitly that `signed_at` is always unique in practice and why.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `most_recent_attestation` breaks `signed_at` ties via 'last processed, matching the legacy mirror's last-writer-wins,' but the legacy mirror's tie-break is by event replay order (file sort by HLC timestamp + UUID prefix), not by `signed_at` value. If two attestation records share an identical `signed_at` (e.g., two rapid sign operations within the same nanosecond resolution), `max()` over `signed_at` has undefined tie-breaking behavior and may return a different record than the legacy mirror did. The plan does not specify whether the helper should use file/event sort order as the secondary sort key to preserve exact behavioral equivalence.
findings.location: What (ordered) — Step 1: `signing.most_recent_attestation(state)` semantics / Acceptance Criteria — AC bullet (b)
findings.suggested_fix: In the helper spec, add: 'ties on signed_at are broken by the record's event sort order (the attestations map value whose source event filename sorts last); in practice signed_at is an HLC nanosecond timestamp that increments per-tick so ties are extremely rare, but the tie-break must be deterministic.' Alternatively, since the map stores only one record per kind (not the full history), and the helper picks the max across kinds, the tie is between records from DIFFERENT kinds — document that in this case the kind chosen is arbitrary (and the existing gate only cares that *some* certified record exists, not which kind).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document whether `most_recent_attestation`'s max(signed_at) ordering is provably equivalent to the legacy mirror's HLC-replay-order last-writer-wins, or explicitly define the cases where they diverge and specify whether the new semantics are intentionally different.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `most_recent_attestation(state)` 'reproduces the mirror's last signature of any kind' by selecting max(signed_at) from `state['attestations']`, but the legacy mirror uses event-file replay order (HLC-encoded filename sort) — not wall-clock signed_at comparison. These two orderings diverge when a record carries a higher signed_at than a later-appended event (e.g. clock skew, out-of-order backfill, or two events from different environments). The tie-breaking rule 'ties → last processed, matching the legacy mirror's last-writer-wins' is also unspecified in implementation terms (dict insertion order? kind-key sort?). This behavioral-equivalence claim is unverified.
findings.location: What section, step 1: 'ADD a NEW helper `signing.most_recent_attestation(state)`'
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is exact behavioral equivalence, implement `most_recent_attestation` as 'the last record inserted into the map across all kinds' (which IS the last-replayed SIGNATURE event of any kindable type) rather than max(signed_at). If the intent is to upgrade semantics to wall-clock order, state that explicitly and add a test demonstrating the intentional divergence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `emit_legacy_signature_mirror: bool = False` to `CompactConfig` and a `lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)` coercer entry under `_SECTIONS['compact']` in `_config_schema.py`, matching the env-var name `REBAR_COMPACT_EMIT_LEGACY_SIGNATURE_MIRROR` via the config loader's env-override path.
findings.finding: `compact.emit_legacy_signature_mirror` is referenced throughout the plan (What step 3, Rollback AC, env var `REBAR_COMPACT_EMIT_LEGACY_SIGNATURE_MIRROR`) but its schema vocabulary — the `CompactConfig` dataclass field and the `_SECTIONS['compact']` coercer entry in `_config_schema.py` — is never defined. Without both additions, the flag is silently treated as an unknown key (warned and ignored) by the existing `_warn_unknown` path, making the rollback mechanism non-functional.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_config_schema.py`: add `emit_legacy_signature_mirror: bool = False` to the `CompactConfig` dataclass, and add `"emit_legacy_signature_mirror": lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)` to `_SECTIONS["compact"]`. Verify that `config.py`'s env-override layer maps `REBAR_COMPACT_EMIT_LEGACY_SIGNATURE_MIRROR` to `compact.emit_legacy_signature_mirror`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a log line (at WARN or INFO level) in `most_recent_attestation` when the `state['signature']` fallback path is taken, so operators can detect and investigate unexpected legacy-snapshot reads post-contract.
findings.finding: The `most_recent_attestation` fallback path (when map is absent/empty, fall back to `state['signature']`) is taken silently with no log, metric, or trace — operators cannot distinguish normal operation from unexpected pre-map snapshot reads after the contract phase ships.
findings.suggested_fix: In `most_recent_attestation`, before returning the fallback value, emit: `logger.warning('most_recent_attestation: attestations map absent/empty, falling back to legacy state[signature]; snapshot may be pre-map', extra={'snapshot_id': ...})`. Document this log in docs/migrations.md as the observable signal that the retirement is not yet complete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a staged rollout path for the `emit_legacy_signature_mirror = false` default — for example, explicitly enabling the drop on a canary subset (e.g., one host or one namespace) for one compaction cycle and verifying before fleet-wide adoption, OR document why the readiness attestation alone is sufficient and what monitoring confirms safety during the rollout window.
findings.finding: The `compact.emit_legacy_signature_mirror` config flag defaults to `false`, meaning the mirror is dropped for 100% of new compactions immediately on deploy — there is no canary percentage, feature-flag gradual rollout, or staged exposure; the only gate is a one-time operator attestation.
findings.location: AC: 'Rollback + named target version' / What Step 3 / `compact.emit_legacy_signature_mirror` flag

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `most_recent_attestation` should be a new function or whether `_record_for_kind(state, kind=None)` should simply be updated in-place to source from `state['attestations']` — since `_record_for_kind` already IS the single kind=None accessor and all three call sites already go through it (directly or via `verify_signature`).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `signing.most_recent_attestation(state)` helper whose semantics (return the record with the greatest `signed_at` from `state['attestations']`, fall back to `state['signature']`) substantially overlap the already-existing private function `signing._record_for_kind(state, kind=None)` at signing.py:552, which today returns `state.get('signature')` and could be evolved in-place to perform the same map-sourced lookup. Introducing a parallel public function while the private one persists creates two access paths to the same concept.
findings.location: Plan §1 ('ADD a NEW helper signing.most_recent_attestation') and §2 ('Migrate … signing._record_for_kind (kind=None verify-latest path)')
findings.suggested_fix: Instead of adding `most_recent_attestation`, update `_record_for_kind(state, kind=None)` to: sort `state['attestations'].values()` by `signed_at` and return the max, falling back to `state.get('signature')`. Rename it to a public name (e.g. `signing.record_for_kind`) only if callers outside `signing.py` need direct access — the plan's three migrated sites are all within or adjacent to `signing.py` anyway.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_processors.py` to the Scope section (as a read-only/no-change dependency) to reconcile the contradiction between 'What' step 4 and the declared Scope.
findings.finding: The 'What' section (step 4) names `_processors.py` as a file that must be preserved ('Keep the read-side fold-in (`_processors.py`) so pre-map snapshots still upgrade on read'), but `_processors.py` is absent from the Scope section. An implementer working strictly from Scope may overlook this file, creating a contradiction between the two sections about what files are in play.
findings.location: What section step 4 vs Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Append `src/rebar/_processors.py` to the Scope list with a parenthetical: '(no change — fold-in must be preserved; verified by AC3(c) round-trip test)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the Why or Step 3 section explaining why the mirror is stripped at compact time (rather than in the reducer processor): specifically, that keeping the reducer processor config-free preserves the reducer layer's dependency-free architecture invariant, and that the config-gated strip at the I/O boundary (compact.py) is the correct seam for a reversible, operationally-controlled schema change.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for the chosen strip-at-compact approach (stripping `signature` from compiled_state in compact.py at snapshot write time, behind a config flag) as opposed to the structurally different alternative of stopping `process_signature` from writing `state['signature']` behind the same flag. The absence of a positive rationale is a G6 rubric gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Step 3: 'The strip is placed in compact.py (not in process_signature) so the reducer layer remains config-free — a dependency-free invariant that compact.py already breaks for its own config reads. This keeps the reducer a pure event→state function and the I/O boundary as the sole gating point for the reversible schema change.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
